concepts.lesson: Area is the measure of the surface covered by a two-dimensional shape. It tells us how much space is inside the boundary of a figure. To measure area, we usually count unit squares that fit inside the shape. The number of such squares gives the area. Area is always measured in square units such as cm², m², etc. Understanding the idea of area helps students compare shapes and determine how much space they occupy.
concepts.real_example: Finding the floor area of a classroom using square tiles.
key_points: Can be visualised using unit squares

concepts.lesson: The area of a rectangle is found by multiplying its length and width. This formula comes from counting the number of rows and columns of unit squares inside the rectangle. A square is a special type of rectangle where all sides are equal. Therefore, its area is calculated by multiplying the side by itself. These formulas are among the most commonly used in geometry and provide the foundation for understanding the areas of more complex shapes.
concepts.real_example: Area of a rectangular garden of length 10 m and width 6 m is 60 m².

concepts.lesson: The area of a triangle is calculated using the formula ½ × base × height. This formula is derived by comparing a triangle with a parallelogram having the same base and height. Two identical triangles can form a parallelogram, whose area is base × height. Therefore, each triangle has half that area. Understanding this relationship helps students visualize geometric transformations and strengthens conceptual understanding.
concepts.real_example: Area of a triangle with base 12 cm and height 8 cm is 48 cm².
key_points: Base and height must be perpendicular

concepts.lesson: The area of a parallelogram is calculated using the formula base × height. Although a parallelogram appears slanted, its area is the same as that of a rectangle having the same base and height. By cutting a triangular part from one side and attaching it to the other side, the parallelogram can be rearranged into a rectangle. This geometric transformation explains why the formula works and helps students understand area beyond memorizing formulas.
concepts.real_example: A parallelogram with base 9 cm and height 7 cm has area 63 cm².

concepts.lesson: The area of a rhombus is calculated using the formula ½ × d1 × d2, where d1 and d2 are the diagonals of the rhombus. The diagonals intersect at right angles and divide the rhombus into four triangles. By rearranging these triangles or by analyzing the geometry of the diagonals, the area formula can be derived. This method shows how properties of diagonals can simplify area calculations.
concepts.real_example: A rhombus with diagonals 10 cm and 12 cm has area 60 cm².

concepts.lesson: A trapezium is a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides. Its area is calculated using the formula ½ × height × (sum of parallel sides). The formula is derived by combining two identical trapeziums to form a parallelogram. This transformation helps students see how the trapezium formula is logically related to the parallelogram area formula.
concepts.real_example: A trapezium with parallel sides 6 cm and 10 cm and height 5 cm has area 40 cm².
key_points: Area = ½ × height × (sum of parallel sides)

concepts.lesson: The area of an irregular polygon can be found by dividing it into simpler shapes such as triangles or rectangles. After dividing the figure, the areas of all the smaller shapes are calculated individually and then added together to obtain the total area. This method is especially useful when dealing with complex shapes that do not have direct formulas.
concepts.real_example: Dividing a pentagon into three triangles to calculate its area.

concepts.lesson: Area is widely used in real life for measuring land, floors, walls, and surfaces. Builders use area to calculate the amount of materials needed for flooring or painting. Farmers use it to determine the size of fields. Engineers and architects rely on area calculations to design buildings and structures efficiently.
concepts.real_example: Calculating the area of a field to determine how much seed is needed for planting.
key_points: Helps measure land and floor space
